title: Supply and the law of supply
summary: Supply is the producer's side of every market. This lesson builds the law of supply from the profit motive and rising marginal cost, shows how to read a supply schedule and curve, and drills the single distinction examiners test most: a movement ALONG the curve (change in quantity supplied) versus a SHIFT of the whole curve (change in supply). It works through the non-price determinants of supply, individual versus market supply, and finishes with a Paper 1 part (a) model answer marked against the real IB markband.
learningObjectives: Define supply and explain the law of supply using a supply schedule and an upward-sloping supply curve.
Explain WHY the supply curve slopes upward, using the profit motive and rising marginal cost.
Distinguish precisely between a movement along the supply curve (change in quantity supplied) and a shift of the supply curve (change in supply).
Identify and explain the non-price determinants of supply and predict the direction of the resulting shift.
Derive market supply from individual producers' supply by horizontal summation.
levelNote: Content is common to SL and HL at this level; HL extensions appear later in the unit.

simpleExplanation.title: Why Firms Sell More When the Price Is High
simpleExplanation.summary: Supply is about producers, not consumers. When the price of a product rises, firms have a stronger incentive to make and sell more of it, because each unit sold is now more profitable — and because they will only cover the higher cost of squeezing out extra output if they receive a higher price.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine you offer a dog-walking service. If neighbours pay £5 a walk you might fit in two walks a week. If a new family offers £15 a walk, you will happily rearrange your evenings to walk more dogs — even giving up gym time to do it. The higher price both rewards you more per walk and makes the extra effort worth its rising cost. Firms reason in exactly the same way.
steps: Supply is the quantity producers are WILLING and ABLE to offer for sale at each price over a period of time.
The law of supply: ceteris paribus, a higher price leads to a higher quantity supplied — a positive relationship.
This gives an upward-sloping supply curve, with Price on the vertical axis and Quantity on the horizontal axis. A change in the good's OWN price is a movement ALONG the curve.
Non-price determinants — costs, technology, taxes, subsidies, expectations, number of firms, shocks — SHIFT the whole curve left or right.

flashcards.back: The quantity of a good or service that producers are WILLING and ABLE to offer for sale at each possible price over a given time period. Willingness is tied to profit; ability is tied to productive capacity.
Ceteris paribus, there is a positive (direct) relationship between the price of a good and its quantity supplied: as price rises, quantity supplied rises; as price falls, quantity supplied falls.

flashcards.front: Why does the supply curve slope UP?
flashcards.back: Two linked reasons. (1) Profit motive: at a higher price, with costs unchanged, each unit earns more, so firms produce more. (2) Rising marginal cost: extra output in the short run costs more per unit (e.g. overtime pay), so firms need a higher price to make it worthwhile.
'Supply' is the WHOLE relationship — the entire curve. 'Quantity supplied' is ONE amount at ONE price — a single point on the curve. Confusing the two is the classic 2.2 error.
Caused ONLY by a change in the good's own price. Correct term: a change in QUANTITY SUPPLIED.
Caused by a change in any NON-PRICE determinant. Correct term: a change in SUPPLY. Rightward = increase; leftward = decrease.
Costs of factors of production; technology; indirect (specific/ad valorem) taxes; subsidies; producer price expectations; number of firms; and supply shocks such as weather for agricultural goods.
Lowers unit production costs, making output more profitable at every price → supply INCREASES → curve shifts RIGHT.
A government payment per unit lowers effective production cost at every output level → supply INCREASES → curve shifts RIGHT (down vertically by the subsidy per unit).
Raises the cost of supplying each unit → supply DECREASES → curve shifts LEFT (up vertically by the tax per unit).
Individual supply is one firm's supply curve. MARKET supply is the HORIZONTAL sum of all individual firms' curves — add the quantities each firm supplies at each price.

The supply curve is, in effect, the firm's marginal cost curve above a minimum: firms supply an extra unit only if its price at least covers the marginal cost of making it. Rising marginal cost is why the curve rises.

quickCheck.prompt: The price of wheat rises and farmers respond by supplying more wheat. This is BEST described as:
options: An increase in supply (rightward shift)
An increase in quantity supplied (movement along the curve)
A decrease in supply (leftward shift)
A change in a non-price determinant
quickCheck.answer: An increase in quantity supplied (movement along the curve)

quickCheck.prompt: Which of the following would shift the supply curve for lattes to the LEFT?
options: A fall in the price of lattes
A rise in the wages of café workers
An improvement in coffee-machine technology
A per-cup subsidy to cafés
quickCheck.answer: A rise in the wages of café workers

quickCheck.prompt: The main reason the supply curve is upward sloping is that:
options: Consumers demand more at lower prices
Higher prices make production more profitable and cover rising marginal costs
Firms always produce as much as possible
Governments require minimum output levels
quickCheck.answer: Higher prices make production more profitable and cover rising marginal costs

quickCheck.prompt: At a price of £3, Firm A supplies 40 units and Firm B supplies 60 units. If these are the only two firms, MARKET supply at £3 is:
options: 20 units
50 units
100 units
Cannot be determined

quickCheck.prompt: A government imposes a specific (per-unit) indirect tax on cigarettes. On the supply diagram this is shown as the supply curve:
options: Shifting right by the tax per unit
Shifting left, vertically up by the tax per unit
A movement up along the existing curve
Becoming perfectly vertical
quickCheck.answer: Shifting left, vertically up by the tax per unit

faq.q: Why is the supply curve upward sloping?
faq.a: Two reasons that reinforce each other. First, the profit motive: at a higher price, with costs unchanged, each unit yields more profit, so firms devote more resources to producing the good. Second, rising marginal cost: to expand output in the short run firms often use more expensive inputs (overtime pay, less efficient machines), so they are only willing to make extra units if the price rises to cover that higher cost.

faq.q: What is the difference between 'a change in supply' and 'a change in quantity supplied'?
faq.a: This is the distinction examiners test most. A 'change in quantity supplied' is a MOVEMENT ALONG the curve caused by the good's OWN price changing. A 'change in supply' is a SHIFT of the whole curve caused by a NON-PRICE determinant such as technology, costs, taxes or subsidies. Using the wrong phrase loses marks even when the economics is otherwise right.

faq.q: If the price of coffee beans rises, does the supply of coffee shift?
faq.a: It depends on which market you mean — a classic trap. In the market for coffee BEANS themselves, a higher bean price raises the QUANTITY SUPPLIED of beans (a movement along the beans' supply curve). But in the market for LATTES, beans are an input: a higher bean price raises cafés' production costs, so the SUPPLY of lattes decreases (a leftward SHIFT). Always identify whose supply curve you are drawing.

faq.q: How do we get from individual supply to market supply?
faq.a: By horizontal summation. At each price, add together the quantities every firm in the market is willing to supply. If at £2 Firm A offers 100 units and Firm B offers 150, market supply at £2 is 250. Repeat at every price and you trace out the market supply curve — which is flatter (more responsive) than any single firm's curve because more producers contribute.

sections.content: Welcome to the other side of the market: the producers. Demand analysis asked what consumers are willing to buy; supply analysis asks what firms are willing to sell. Why do smartphone makers ship more units when a new model commands a high price? Why does a farmer plant more of one crop this year than last? Why does a new tax on a product shrink the amount reaching the shelves? All of these come down to supply — and to one distinction, movements versus shifts, that examiners return to again and again.

In economics, supply is a precise idea, not just 'how much exists'. **Supply** is the quantity of a good or service that a producer is both **willing** and **able** to offer for sale at each possible price, over a particular time period. 'Willing' points to the profit motive — firms supply because they expect to gain; 'able' points to productive capacity — they must actually have the resources to make the good. Both conditions must hold: a firm that would love to sell but cannot produce, or could produce but sees no profit, supplies nothing.
The law of supply and the supply curve

The **law of supply** states that, *ceteris paribus* (all other factors held constant), as the price of a good rises the quantity supplied rises, and as the price falls the quantity supplied falls. Price and quantity supplied move in the SAME direction — a positive relationship — which is why the supply curve slopes upward. By convention Price is on the vertical axis and Quantity is on the horizontal axis, and the curve is labelled S.

items: **Positive relationship:** price and quantity supplied move in the same direction, giving an upward-sloping curve.
**Profit motive:** at a higher price, with costs unchanged, each unit is more profitable, so firms allocate more resources to producing the good.

**Rising marginal cost:** producing each extra unit in the short run tends to cost more (overtime, less-suitable inputs). Firms will only incur that rising cost if the price rises to match — so higher output requires a higher price.

**Marginal-cost intuition:** a firm supplies one more unit only when its price at least covers the marginal cost of making it. The supply curve is effectively the marginal cost curve — which is exactly why it rises.

sections.question:
A local bakery's supply schedule for croissants is shown below.

| Price per croissant (£) | Quantity supplied per day |
|---|---|
| 1.00 | 50 |
| 1.50 | 100 |
| 2.00 | 150 |
| 2.50 | 200 |
| 3.00 | 250 |

(a) Describe how you would draw the supply curve and state its shape.
(b) If the market price rises from £1.50 to £2.50, describe the change precisely and explain what causes it.
sections.solution:
**(a) Drawing the curve:**
1. Put Price (£) on the vertical axis and Quantity supplied per day on the horizontal axis.
2. Choose a scale: price from 0 to about 3.50, quantity from 0 to about 300.
3. Plot each pair from the schedule — (50, 1.00), (100, 1.50), (150, 2.00), (200, 2.50), (250, 3.00).
4. Join the points and label the line **S**. Because equal £0.50 price rises raise quantity by a constant 50 units, the result is an **upward-sloping straight line**.

**(b) Describing the change:**
1. At £1.50 the bakery supplies 100 croissants; at £2.50 it supplies 200.
2. This is an **increase in QUANTITY SUPPLIED** from 100 to 200 per day — NOT an increase in supply.
3. It is shown as an **upward movement ALONG the existing curve S**, caused solely by the rise in the croissant's own price. The curve itself does not move, because no non-price determinant has changed.

sections.content: Movements along vs. shifts of the supply curve

This is the single most tested distinction in microeconomics. A change in the price of the good ITSELF causes a **movement along** the supply curve — a *change in quantity supplied*. A change in any OTHER factor affecting a producer's willingness or ability to sell causes the whole curve to **shift** — a *change in supply*. Getting the language right is worth marks in itself.

items: **Movement along the curve:** caused ONLY by a change in the good's own price → 'change in quantity supplied'.
**Shift of the curve:** caused by a change in a non-price determinant → 'change in supply'.
**Increase in supply (rightward shift):** firms supply MORE at every price — e.g. a new, more efficient technology lowers costs.
**Decrease in supply (leftward shift):** firms supply LESS at every price — e.g. a new tax or a rise in input costs.

sections.content: Be surgical with language. 'Supply increases' means the whole curve shifts (a non-price cause). 'Quantity supplied increases' means a movement along the curve (a price cause). Writing 'supply rises' when the price rose — or drawing a shift when you mean a movement — costs marks even if the rest of your answer is correct. Say the exact phrase, then match it to the exact diagram move.

These are the factors held constant by *ceteris paribus*. When one of them changes, the supply curve shifts. A useful memory aid is that almost all of them work by changing the **cost** or **profitability** of producing each unit — lower cost shifts supply right, higher cost shifts it left.

items: **Costs of factors of production:** higher wages, rent, or raw-material prices raise unit costs → supply DECREASES (shifts left). Lower input costs shift it right.
**Technology:** an improvement lowers unit costs and raises productivity → supply INCREASES (shifts right).
**Indirect taxes:** a specific or ad valorem tax raises the cost of supplying each unit → supply DECREASES (shifts left, vertically up by the tax per unit).
**Subsidies:** a per-unit government payment lowers effective cost → supply INCREASES (shifts right, vertically down by the subsidy per unit).
**Producer price expectations:** if firms expect a much higher future price, they may withhold stock now to sell later → current supply DECREASES (shifts left).
**Number of firms:** more firms entering raises market supply (shifts right); firms leaving reduces it (shifts left).
**Supply shocks / weather:** for agricultural and commodity goods, good weather raises supply (shifts right); droughts, floods or disasters reduce it (shifts left).

sections.question:
The market for electric cars is initially at supply curve S1. The government introduces a £2,000 subsidy to manufacturers for each electric car produced.

Using a supply diagram, explain the effect on the supply of electric cars, and state precisely how the shift is drawn.
sections.solution:
**Economic reasoning:**
1. A subsidy is a per-unit payment from the government to producers, so it lowers the effective cost of producing each electric car.
2. With lower unit costs, producing electric cars is more profitable at every selling price.
3. Firms therefore offer more at each price — an **increase in SUPPLY** (a change in supply, not merely quantity supplied, because the cause is a non-price determinant).

**Diagram (described):**
1. Draw Price (£) on the vertical axis and Quantity of electric cars on the horizontal axis.
2. Draw the original upward-sloping supply curve **S1**.
3. Draw a new curve **S2 to the RIGHT of S1**. Because the subsidy is £2,000 per car, S2 lies **vertically £2,000 below S1** at every quantity — the government now covers £2,000 of each unit's cost.
4. Add an arrow from S1 to S2 to show the direction of the shift.

**Conclusion:** The subsidy shifts supply right from S1 to S2. At any given price a greater quantity of electric cars is now supplied — an increase in supply, not a movement along the curve.

sections.content: Everything so far describes one firm's supply. **Market supply** is the total that ALL producers in a market offer, found by **horizontal summation** — at each price, add up the quantities every firm is willing to supply. Because more producers contribute at every price, the market supply curve is flatter and further to the right than any single firm's curve.

sections.question:
A market for handmade candles has just two producers. Their individual supply schedules are:

| Price (£) | Firm A quantity | Firm B quantity |
|---|---|---|
| 2 | 10 | 20 |
| 4 | 30 | 40 |
| 6 | 50 | 60 |

(a) Derive the market supply schedule.
(b) A third producer, Firm C, now enters and supplies 15 candles at £2, 25 at £4 and 35 at £6. State what happens to the market supply curve and why.
sections.solution:
**(a) Horizontal summation** — add the two quantities at each price:
- At £2: 10 + 20 = **30 candles**.
- At £4: 30 + 40 = **70 candles**.
- At £6: 50 + 60 = **110 candles**.
The market supply schedule is therefore (£2, 30), (£4, 70), (£6, 110) — an upward-sloping market supply curve.

**(b) A new firm entering** adds to the quantity supplied at every price:
- At £2: 30 + 15 = 45; at £4: 70 + 25 = 95; at £6: 110 + 35 = 145.
The increase in the NUMBER OF FIRMS is a non-price determinant, so market **supply INCREASES** — the market supply curve **shifts to the RIGHT**. Note that Firm A's and Firm B's own curves are unchanged; only the market total shifts.

items: **Confusing 'supply' with 'quantity supplied'** — 'supply' is the whole curve; 'quantity supplied' is one point. Use 'change in quantity supplied' for price-driven movements and 'change in supply' for non-price shifts.
**Drawing a shift when the cause is the good's OWN price (or vice versa)** — a price change is ALWAYS a movement along the curve, never a shift.
**Getting the shift direction wrong for taxes and subsidies** — a tax RAISES cost so supply shifts LEFT; a subsidy LOWERS cost so supply shifts RIGHT. Weak answers reverse these.
**Mislabelling the axes** — Price MUST be on the vertical axis and Quantity on the horizontal. An unlabelled or reversed diagram cannot reach the top band.
**Forgetting *ceteris paribus*** — the law of supply only holds with other factors constant; stating this shows the examiner you understand the assumption.

**Confusing the beans-vs-lattes trap** — a change in an INPUT price shifts the supply of the FINISHED good, but changes the quantity supplied of the input itself. Always identify whose supply curve you are analysing.

sections.content: Model answer — marked against the real IB markband

sections.question: Paper 1, part (a): Explain, using a supply diagram, the law of supply and TWO non-price determinants that could shift the supply curve. [10 marks]
sections.solution:
**Model answer:**
The law of supply states that, ceteris paribus, as the price of a good rises its quantity supplied rises, and as price falls quantity supplied falls — a positive relationship between price and quantity supplied. This gives an upward-sloping supply curve. Two reasons explain the slope: the profit motive (a higher price with unchanged costs makes each unit more profitable, so firms produce more) and rising marginal cost (extra output in the short run costs more per unit, so firms need a higher price to supply it).

*[Diagram: Price on the vertical axis, Quantity on the horizontal axis. An upward-sloping supply curve labelled S1. A movement from point X to point Y ALONG S1 shows the law of supply — a rise in price from P1 to P2 raises quantity supplied from Q1 to Q2. A second curve S2 is drawn to the RIGHT of S1 to show an increase in supply, and a third curve S0 to the LEFT to show a decrease in supply.]*

A change in the good's OWN price causes a movement ALONG the curve from X to Y — a change in QUANTITY SUPPLIED — illustrating the law of supply. In contrast, non-price determinants SHIFT the whole curve. First, technology: an improvement lowers unit production costs, making output more profitable at every price, so supply increases and the curve shifts RIGHT from S1 to S2. Second, an indirect tax: a per-unit tax raises the cost of supplying each unit, so supply decreases and the curve shifts LEFT from S1 to S0 (vertically up by the tax per unit). Both are 'changes in supply', distinct from the price-driven 'change in quantity supplied'.

---
**How this maps to our IB markband (Paper 1 part (a), out of 10):**
- **9–10:** demands of the question addressed, relevant economic theory FULLY explained, relevant terms used appropriately THROUGHOUT, and where appropriate a relevant diagram included and FULLY explained.
- **7–8:** relevant economic theory explained, relevant terms mostly used appropriately, and where appropriate a relevant diagram included and explained.
- **5–6:** question partially addressed, relevant economic theory PARTLY explained, some relevant terms used, and where appropriate a relevant diagram included.
- **3–4:** relevant economic theory DESCRIBED, some relevant terms used.
- **1–2:** the answer is not relevant to the question.

Why this response reaches **9–10**:
- ✅ Addresses every demand — the law of supply AND two named non-price determinants.
- ✅ Theory FULLY explained: the slope is derived from profit motive and marginal cost, and each determinant's effect is explained through cost and profitability, not merely stated.
- ✅ Terms used appropriately THROUGHOUT — 'quantity supplied', 'change in supply', 'ceteris paribus', 'non-price determinant', 'indirect tax'.
- ✅ Diagram included and FULLY explained: axes labelled, the X→Y movement tied to the law of supply, and the S1→S2 / S1→S0 shifts tied to the two determinants.
A response that only DESCRIBED the law of supply and listed two determinants with an unexplained or unlabelled diagram would sit in the 3–4 ('theory described') or 5–6 ('partly explained') band. Explaining the diagram rather than just drawing it is exactly what lifts an answer into the top band.

sections.content: This is exactly what the Practice button does: write your own answer to this question, submit it, and our marking engine grades it against this SAME official markband — telling you which band you reached and why. The habit that moves you up the bands: name the two non-price determinants, EXPLAIN each shift through cost and profitability, and fully label AND explain your diagram rather than just drawing it.

sections.label: Get a Paper 1 (a) answer marked: use a supply diagram to explain the law of supply and two non-price determinants
